canonicalExplanation.summary: Python names bind to objects; core scalar values include arbitrary-precision integers, finite-precision floats, booleans, None, and immutable Unicode strings, each with distinct equality and truth rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A missing optional value is checked with is None, a money amount uses Decimal at the boundary, and a count remains int without assuming a fixed machine width. Your goal is to choose and operate on suitable scalar values while explaining precision and absence semantics. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scripts, indentation and main guard; pf state expressions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python names bind to objects; core scalar values include arbitrary-precision integers, finite-precision floats, booleans, None, and immutable Unicode strings, each with distinct equality and truth rules. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: bool is an int subclass, float cannot exactly represent many decimals, NaN has unusual equality, None is a singleton absence marker, and truthiness does not mean semantic validity. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Python floats store decimal values exactly and any falsey value is equivalent to None.

includes: Use core immutable scalar values and distinguish type, value and truthiness.
Choose and operate on suitable scalar values while explaining precision and absence semantics.
excludes: bool is an int subclass, float cannot exactly represent many decimals, NaN has unusual equality, None is a singleton absence marker, and truthiness does not mean semantic validity.

outcomes: Choose and operate on suitable scalar values while explaining precision and absence semantics.

examples.title: Numbers, booleans, None and text: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: An empty string is false but valid input for one domain, so code checks the actual contract instead of rejecting every falsey value.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: An empty string is false but valid input for one domain, so code checks the actual contract instead of rejecting every falsey value.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: bool is an int subclass, float cannot exactly represent many decimals, NaN has unusual equality, None is a singleton absence marker, and truthiness does not mean semantic validity.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Python floats store decimal values exactly and any falsey value is equivalent to None.
